text: Chapter 7 elaborates the **bebop scale** with three recognition and pedagogy rules. First, intensified bebop-scale use historically begins in the Bebop Era (1940-1950) but the device predates that period — Coker cites a 1927 Armstrong phrase on "Hotter Than That" — so students should hear it across style periods. Second, a player need not deploy the entire scale to be "using" it: a 3-4 note fragment containing the designated chromatic note already implies bebop-scale use, which dramatically expands recognition and application. Third, the chapter quantifies saturation: Coltrane plays the bebop-scale pattern 21 times in an 8-chorus solo on a 16-bar tune, showing the scale operating as a structural device, not an ornament. Coker dispenses with the customary written illustrations and routes the student directly to recorded examples and to Baker's HOW TO PLAY BEBOP, then specifies cycle-of-fifths transposition practice (Track N) and a critical track-alignment warning so the student does not start exercises in the wrong key against the backing tracks.
